episode_meta.title: Natali Helberger: Audience-Centric Approaches to AI Ethics in Media

guests.name: Natali Helberger
guests.role: Professor of Law and Digital Technology; Co-founder, AI, Media and Democracy Lab; Chair, Council of Europe Expert Group on AI and Freedom of Expression

use_cases.outcome: Hesitancy due to real-time character and difficulty checking output; earlier models produced nonsensical output

use_cases.description: Newsrooms using AI for headline generation, writing article summaries, analyzing documents, and analyzing data
use_cases.outcome: Becoming embedded in journalistic workflows with editorial checkpoint as final step

use_cases.description: Using AI for transcription and translation in newsrooms
use_cases.outcome: Considered just a tool to make content better; no explicit transparency disclosure needed
Users are concerned about whether content was produced by AI in this context

use_cases.description: Meta labeling AI-generated content with 'made with AI' labels
use_cases.outcome: First step in transparency but does not tell users enough or solve the deepfake issue

use_cases.description: Using generative AI to create images for presentations instead of stock photos
Developing diversity recommender metrics to diversify news recommendation algorithms
use_cases.outcome: Developed metrics for fragmentation, activation, and representation to improve quality of public debate

use_cases.description: Panopticum project exploring design choices to let users express real preferences rather than relying on inferred preferences
use_cases.outcome: Exploring easy design choices for user preference expression

key_claims.claim: Nearly 20% of newsrooms surveyed said their strategy for ethical use of AI was simply not to use AI
key_claims.speaker: Nikita Roy (citing Associated Press / Helberger / Diakopoulos study)

key_claims.claim: The major ethical concern among newsrooms was the lack of human oversight, and there is a strong belief that as long as a human is watching over AI, it is more trustworthy
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: The least popular strategy among newsrooms was to audit the systems or do responsible procurement
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: Ecological concerns and the role of big tech companies were almost not mentioned in the survey responses despite being prominent in public discourse
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: In Europe, there was less empirical evidence for filter bubbles materializing to a significant extent, partly because people use multiple outlets
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: Being transparent alone does not necessarily increase trust of people; they also want agency and choice
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: 60% of respondents in the Netherlands said they would feel misled if something was generated by AI and they were not informed about it
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

key_claims.claim: Users trust in news media using AI and algorithms responsibly is in some countries on par with their trust in social media using them responsibly
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger (citing Reuters Institute report)

key_claims.claim: Users would very much appreciate being included in the human oversight loop and having the opportunity to complain or flag issues
key_claims.speaker: Natali Helberger

challenges.description: Nearly 20% of newsrooms avoid AI entirely due to ethical concerns, inhibiting innovation
Lack of human oversight over AI systems is the top ethical concern, but humans cannot detect all issues (e.g., biases in training data) just by looking at output
AI output problems: inaccurate information, biases, hallucinations, lack of quality in content
AI implementation in newsrooms is not only a technical problem but also an ethical and cultural problem
Liability for AI failures is dumped on end users/professional users in providers' terms of use
Smaller newsrooms lack legal departments and find it difficult to know what to look for when procuring AI technology
It is difficult or impossible to reach large AI providers for support, especially for smaller newsrooms
Transparency labeling (e.g., 'made with AI') is a regulation of the willing; those producing deepfakes to derail elections will not voluntarily label their content
Filter bubble and polarization concerns with news recommendation systems, especially for users who only receive news via TikTok or Instagram
Explainability of AI models exists in theory but there is less work on making explainability actionable and meaningful for people
Label blindness: users will be exposed to increasing numbers of AI labels and may eventually stop paying attention
Enforcement of the EU AI Act rules in practice remains a major challenge
Responsible AI guidelines are often just pledges on paper and need to be translated into actual working practices, skills, routines, funding, and incentives

quotes.text: AI and the implementation of AI in newsrooms is often talked about as a technical problem. But I think this survey also very much showed it's not only a technical problem, it's also an ethical and a cultural problem.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger

quotes.text: Someone who's a party here that is at the moment completely excluded from the loop are, for example, users. And their ability to feedback or to criticize or to let the media organization know when something goes wrong. And in our research we actually saw that users would very much appreciate being included in this loop.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger

quotes.text: Being transparent about what parameter you use is very nice and good, but it starts only being really meaningful if you also give people a choice maybe between different parameters or recommendation logics.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger
quotes.topic: Transparency and user agency in recommendation systems

quotes.text: I think we really need to think of how to distinguish the media and make sure that people know if I want trustworthy content, I go there because it is fact-checked, there's human oversight, someone has been testing the system, there was responsible procurement, they are transparent about it.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger
quotes.topic: Differentiating news media from social media platforms

quotes.text: Responsible use of AI is not simply something that you can write up in a guideline and that it will automatically materialize, but one also really needs to think of how that should be then internalized in working practices, in skills, in new routines.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger
quotes.topic: Implementing ethical AI guidelines in practice

quotes.text: Simply the fact that something has been made with AI does not necessarily say that this is bad content. So I think it's a good first step. But I also think it doesn't tell you enough. So I don't think it will solve the deep fake issue.
quotes.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Use a procurement checklist when acquiring AI tools, covering data quality, data security, training data use, liability, explainability, auditability, support availability, pricing, and ecological footprint
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger
recommendations.target_audience: newsrooms, especially smaller ones without large legal departments

recommendations.advice: Include users/audience in the human-in-the-loop process by giving them the ability to provide feedback, complain, or flag issues with AI-generated content
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Be transparent about AI use when it has significant journalistic impact and is audience-facing, especially when directly generating content; less critical for back-end tools like transcription or translation
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Give users agency and choice over recommendation systems, including the ability to choose between different recommendation logics and periodically review/update their profiles
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger
recommendations.target_audience: media organizations and recommendation system designers

recommendations.advice: Distinguish news media from social media by emphasizing fact-checking, human oversight, responsible procurement, and transparency about AI use to build audience trust
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Do not start ethical guidelines from scratch; learn from the many existing guidelines that are converging on shared principles like transparency, human oversight, data privacy, and source protection
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Be specific about the type of AI tool applied in transparency disclosures, not just 'made with AI' but which tool and to what end, and harmonize the industry's language around generative AI
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger (citing Nordic AI initiative)

recommendations.advice: Optimize news recommendation algorithms for diversity metrics including fragmentation, activation, and representation rather than just engagement and clicks
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

recommendations.advice: Ask users what kind of information they need about AI use rather than assuming; conduct focus groups to understand audience expectations
recommendations.speaker: Natali Helberger

external_references: Associated Press and Northwestern University survey on AI strategies in newsrooms (Helberger and Nick Diakopoulos, 2024)
AI, Media and Democracy Lab (University of Amsterdam)
Council of Europe Expert Group on AI and Freedom of Expression
Reuters Institute for Journalism at Oxford
Nordic AI initiative (principles on AI transparency in journalism)
Reuters Institute report on audience attitudes towards algorithms
Panopticum project (user preference design in recommender systems)
Council of Europe committee on responsible use of AI in journalism
Bipolis study (AI as cultural problem in newsrooms)
